description: This figure presents a data plot showing the room temperature microhardness (HU, universal hardness in N/mm²) of (111) oriented Fe-16%Al monocrystals as a function of various prior heat treatments. The x-axis displays different thermal histories including as-grown conditions, furnace cooling from 1100°C to room temperature (FC RT), interrupted furnace cooling to 600°C followed by water quench (FC 600°C CQ), and various annealing treatments at 250°C, 300°C, 320°C, 400°C, and 600°C for different durations ranging from 6 hours to 223 days. Additionally, the dataset includes water-quenched specimens from 1100°C (denoted Q*) and quenched-plus-annealed samples (Q*+300°C/14d). The data points, presented with error bars, indicate hardness values ranging approximately from 1870 to 2100 N/mm². The chart reveals that hardness is relatively insensitive to heat treatment variations, changing by at most 10%. Notably, the highest hardness (~2100 N/mm²) is observed for the quenched condition (Q*), which caption states is attributed to excess vacancies trapped during quenching, while extended annealing at 300°C and higher temperatures results in slightly reduced hardness values. This supports the paper's conclusion that short-range atomic ordering has no major impact on hardness, whereas excess vacancies contribute significantly to hardening.

Figure 2 presents compressive stress-strain curves for [112]-oriented Fe-16%Al monocrystals tested at room temperature with a compression rate of 10^-4 s^-1, comparing the effects of four distinct prior heat treatments. The four curves represent: (1) as-grown material without additional heat treatment, (2) furnace cooling (FC) from 1100°C to room temperature, (3) furnace cooling from 1100°C to 600°C followed by water quenching (FC 600°C Q), and (4) water quenching from 1100°C followed by annealing at 300°C for 14 days (1100°C Q + 300°C/14d). Visual analysis reveals that the as-grown, FC, and FC 600°C Q specimens exhibit similar yield strengths around 400-430 MPa and comparable strain-hardening behaviors. In contrast, the quenched-and-annealed specimen (1100°C Q + 300°C/14d) displays markedly higher yield stress (~450-480 MPa) and elevated flow stress throughout the plastic deformation regime. These results support the conclusion that only heat treatments involving temperatures above 600°C significantly affect the mechanical behavior of Fe-Al alloys, likely due to variations in vacancy concentrations and short-range ordering kinetics.

This figure presents compressive yield stress data for polycrystalline as-cast Fe-Al alloys as a function of aluminum content (0–18 at.%) at room temperature. The plot contains two primary data series distinguished by symbol type: open circles represent measurements at a strain rate of 10⁻² s⁻¹, while filled circles denote tests conducted at 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹. Both datasets exhibit a linear increase of yield stress with increasing Al content, with interpolating lines indicating hardening coefficients of approximately 21 MPa per at.% for the higher strain rate and 23 MPa per at.% for the lower rate. A distinct open triangle symbol marks the Fe-18%Al alloy, which caption states yielded with concurrent deformation twinning at both compression rates. The yield stress ranges from approximately 50 MPa for pure iron to over 400 MPa for Fe-18%Al. This linear solid-solution hardening behavior supports Suzuki's theory for bcc alloys, demonstrating that the strength increase is produced exclusively by solid solution hardening without significant contribution from short-range ordering in the as-cast condition.

Figure 4 presents tensile stress-strain curves for as-cast polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys with varying aluminum contents (4–18 at.% Al) tested at room temperature (RT) and −100 °C with a strain rate of 10^−4 s^−1. The curves demonstrate that increasing Al content significantly increases yield strength and ultimate tensile strength while progressively reducing fracture strain. At room temperature, all alloys exhibit ductile fracture behavior, whereas at −100 °C, higher Al contents (particularly above 11–14 at.%) show increasingly brittle fracture characteristics. Notable features include coarse serrations in the Fe-18at%Al curve at room temperature, attributed to deformation twinning as confirmed by metallographic observations, and a distinct yield stress drop in Fe-4at%Al at −100 °C. The figure illustrates the ductile-to-brittle transition with increasing Al content and decreasing temperature, supporting the conclusion that dislocation mobility decreases with higher Al concentrations, promoting deformation twinning at low temperatures.

The figure presents tensile mechanical properties of as-cast polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys (4–18 at.% Al) tested at a strain rate of 10^-4 s^-1, comparing room temperature (a) and -100°C (b). Four metrics are plotted against Al content: tensile yield stress (filled circles), ultimate tensile strength (filled triangles), strain before necking (open triangles), and fracture strain (open circles). Additionally, Fig. 5(b) includes the stress for initiating deformation twinning (filled triangles) at -100°C. Both yield stress and ultimate tensile strength exhibit a linear increase with Al content due to solid-solution hardening, with values at -100°C being approximately 80 MPa higher than at room temperature. Conversely, ductility metrics decrease with increasing Al content, showing a steep decline above 15% Al at room temperature and above 12% Al at -100°C, indicating a ductile-to-brittle transition. Deformation twinning occurs at -100°C for Al contents above 11%. The data support Suzuki's theory of solid-solution hardening for bcc alloys.

Transmission electron micrographs illustrating dislocation distributions in polycrystalline Fe-12 at.% Al alloy (60 ppm C) following compressive deformation at room temperature. Subfigure (a) depicts the water-quenched condition from 1100 °C, showing distinct glide bands separated by regions containing dense dislocation tangles after 5.5% compressive strain applied at a strain rate of 10^-4 s^-1. Subfigure (b) shows the furnace-cooled counterpart, exhibiting organized dislocation cell structures with high-density cell walls and relatively clear cell interiors under identical deformation conditions. This comparison reveals that cooling rate profoundly affects deformation substructure development: water quenching suppresses dislocation rearrangement into cells, maintaining planar slip characteristics, while furnace cooling permits dynamic recovery and cell formation. These microstructural differences correlate with the reported weak quenching effect in this alloy composition, contrasting with the strong quenching sensitivity observed in higher aluminum content alloys where quenching preserves different dislocation morphologies.

Transmission electron micrographs showing dislocation distributions in polycrystalline Fe-16%Al alloy (containing less than 20 ppm C) after 5.5% compressive straining at room temperature with a strain rate of 10^-4 s^-1. Subfigure (a) presents the microstructure of the water-quenched specimen from 1100°C, revealing glide bands extending in various directions with primarily straight dislocations situated between them. Subfigure (b) shows the furnace-cooled specimen, exhibiting dense dislocation tangles between the glide bands. The marked contrast between the two heat treatment conditions demonstrates that quenching preserves excess vacancies and suppresses short-range ordering, facilitating dislocation glide and resulting in straighter dislocation configurations, whereas furnace cooling allows vacancy equilibration and short-range order development, leading to dislocation immobilization and tangling. This figure provides direct TEM evidence for the pronounced effect of quenching on deformation mechanisms in Fe-16%Al alloys, correlating thermal history with dislocation substructure evolution and mechanical behavior.
